competitors.label: MDN Type coercion
competitors.selectionReason: MDN's glossary entry is the canonical docs baseline for the coercion concept.
theirStrengths: It gives the authoritative definition of coercion and links the concept back to the language model.
theirGaps: It is narrower than our page on interview framing and default operator guidance.

realWorldUseCases: The real production bugs show up at boundaries: form fields, query params, localStorage, and API payloads often arrive as strings even when business logic expects numbers or booleans.
MDN defines the concept authoritatively, but it is lighter on UI-boundary bugs and production parsing mistakes.
actionableExamples: The glossary is concise, while our page now includes concrete conversion snippets and a checkout-boundary scenario.
followUpConfusion: MDN remains a strong conceptual reference, so the broader follow-up comparison stays close.
Add one short note on ToPrimitive ordering or Symbol.toPrimitive to close the docs-level follow-up gap.

competitors.winReason: Our page now goes beyond the glossary definition with boundary bugs, conversion code, and operator-specific traps, while MDN remains a conceptual baseline.

competitors.label: GreatFrontEnd == vs ===
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd does not appear to have a full type-coercion page, but this equality question is directly relevant because our trivia heavily emphasizes coercion through == vs ===.
theirStrengths: It targets one of the most common interview manifestations of coercion directly.
theirGaps: It is narrower than a full coercion explanation because it is centered on equality operators.

realWorldUseCases: The real production bugs show up at boundaries: form fields, query params, localStorage, and API payloads often arrive as strings even when business logic expects numbers or booleans.
actionableExamples: A checkout form sends quantity as '0', a promo flag as 'false', and a query param as '18'. If you compare or validate them without explicit conversion, the UI can accept bad data or apply the wrong rule.

realWorldUseCases: GreatFrontEnd maps well to equality confusion, but our page now covers several real boundary bugs outside == versus ===.
actionableExamples: Its operator examples are useful, while our page now adds boundary parsing code and concrete boolean/string traps.
followUpConfusion: The equality-focused interview follow-up remains competitive, so this criterion stays close.
Add one explicit Symbol.toPrimitive follow-up so the advanced coercion story is stronger than an equality-only comparison.

competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is strong on equality, but our page now covers coercion across form input, booleans, object conversion, and fallback operators instead of only one operator family.

competitors.label: GeeksforGeeks Type coercion in JavaScript
competitors.selectionReason: This is a mainstream tutorial-style page for the exact coercion search intent.
theirStrengths: It is optimized for the exact topic query and usually includes several concrete conversion examples.
theirGaps: It is often more definitional than production-oriented.

realWorldUseCases: The real production bugs show up at boundaries: form fields, query params, localStorage, and API payloads often arrive as strings even when business logic expects numbers or booleans.
GeeksforGeeks answers the exact topic, but our page now ties the concept more directly to boundary bugs in frontend flows.
actionableExamples: Its examples are useful, while our page now layers equality cases, boundary parsing, and explicit conversion guidance together.
followUpConfusion: The follow-up comparison is still close because the concept family overlaps heavily.
Add one short note on object-to-primitive coercion hooks so the advanced follow-up set is harder to beat.

competitors.winReason: Our page now combines boundary bugs, explicit conversion code, and high-signal operator follow-ups, while GeeksforGeeks stays more introductory.
